preamble.summary: Psalm 120 is the first of the fifteen Songs of Ascents (Psalms 120-134), a collection traditionally associated with pilgrimage to Jerusalem. The psalmist cries out to the LORD from a place of exile among deceitful, war-loving people. He describes himself as living in Meshech and dwelling among the tents of Kedar — both far-flung locations that represent hostile foreign territory. The psalm is a lament of displacement: the psalmist longs for peace but is surrounded by those who want war.
preamble.remarkable: The Songs of Ascents (shir ha-ma'alot) are the 'pilgrimage playlist' of ancient Israel — songs sung by worshipers as they traveled up to Jerusalem for the three annual festivals. Psalm 120 begins the journey at the point of maximum distance from Jerusalem, both geographically and spiritually. The psalmist is among liars and warmongers, far from the peace of Zion. The collection will gradually move closer to Jerusalem and the Temple, arriving at the priestly blessing in Psalm 134. The structure of the entire collection enacts the journey from exile to worship. Beginning with a lament is significant: the pilgrimage does not start from a place of strength but from a place of suffering.
preamble.friction: Meshech was a region in Anatolia (modern Turkey), and Kedar was an Arabian desert tribe — placing the psalmist simultaneously in both locations is geographically impossible. Most scholars understand these as metaphorical: Meshech and Kedar represent the far north and the far south, together meaning 'I am as far from Jerusalem as it is possible to be.' The psalm's language about lying lips and deceitful tongues suggests the psalmist's primary suffering is social rather than physical — he is surrounded by people who cannot be trusted.
preamble.connections: Psalm 120 begins the Songs of Ascents (120-134), a coherent collection with its own internal progression. The theme of lying tongues connects to Psalms 12, 52, and 140. Meshech appears in Genesis 10:2 and Ezekiel 38-39 as a distant, threatening people. Kedar appears in Song of Solomon 1:5, Isaiah 21:16-17, and Jeremiah 49:28-29 as desert-dwelling warriors. The longing for shalom ('peace') in verse 7 anticipates Psalm 122:6-8, where the pilgrim prays for the peace of Jerusalem.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁ֗יר הַֽמַּ֫עֲל֥וֹת אֶל־יְ֭הוָה בַּצָּרָ֣תָה לִּ֑י קָ֝רָ֗אתִי וַֽיַּעֲנֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: In my distress I cried unto the LORD, and he heard me.
verses.rendering:
A Song of Ascents.
In my distress I called to the LORD,
and He answered me.
verses.expanded_rendering: Shir ha-ma'alot ('a song of the ascents') is the superscription shared by all fifteen psalms in this collection (120-134). The word ma'alot means 'ascents, steps, goings up' — referring either to the physical ascent to Jerusalem (which sits on a hill) or to the steps of the Temple, or to the spiritual ascent of the pilgrim's journey. The psalm then begins: el YHWH batzaratah li qarati vayyaaneni ('to the LORD in my distress I called, and He answered me'). The verb qarati ('I called') is past tense — the deliverance has already happened. The psalmist testifies to what God has done, establishing the pattern for the entire pilgrimage collection: the journey begins with a testimony of answered prayer.
translator_notes: The superscription shir ha-ma'alot appears on all fifteen psalms of this collection. The word ma'alot comes from the root alah ('to go up, to ascend'). The phrase batzaratah li ('in my distress, in my narrow place') uses tzarah ('distress, trouble, tight place'), the same word family as metzar in Psalm 118:5. The pilgrimage begins from a place of constriction.

key_terms.semantic_range: ascents, steps, stairs, goings up, degrees
key_terms.note: ma'alot refers to the act of going upward. Applied to these psalms, it describes the pilgrimage journey 'up' to Jerusalem — a journey that is simultaneously geographical (ascending the Judean hills), liturgical (ascending the Temple steps), and spiritual (ascending from distress to praise).

verses.text_hebrew: יְֽהוָ֗ה הַצִּ֣ילָה נַ֭פְשִׁי מִשְּׂפַת־שֶׁ֑קֶר מִלָּשׁ֥וֹן רְמִיָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Deliver my soul, O LORD, from lying lips, and from a deceitful tongue.
translator_notes: The prayer hatzilah nafshi ('rescue my life') is directed against sfat sheqer ('lips of falsehood') and lashon remiyyah ('tongue of deceit'). The psalmist's primary enemy is not a sword but a mouth. The suffering described throughout this psalm is social and verbal — he is surrounded by people whose words are weapons. The word remiyyah ('deceit, treachery') suggests calculated deception, not casual dishonesty.

verses.text_hebrew: מַה־יִּתֵּ֣ן לְ֭ךָ וּמַה־יֹּסִ֥יף לָ֗ךְ לָשׁ֥וֹן רְמִיָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: What shall be given unto thee? or what shall be done unto thee, thou false tongue?
verses.rendering:
What will He give you,
and what more will He add to you,
you deceitful tongue?
verses.expanded_rendering: Mah yitten lekha umah yossif lakh lashon remiyyah ('what will He give you, and what will He add to you, O deceitful tongue?') — the psalmist addresses the tongue directly, as if cross-examining it. The question echoes a formula used in oath-taking: 'May God do thus to you and more also...' (1 Samuel 3:17, 2 Samuel 3:35). The implication is that God will impose a penalty on the deceitful tongue, and the question is: what will that penalty be? The answer comes in the next verse.
translator_notes: The formula mah yitten lekha umah yossif lakh parallels the oath formula koh ya'aseh lekha Elohim ve-koh yossif ('thus may God do to you and thus may He add'). By using this pattern, the psalmist implies that the deceitful tongue is under divine oath-curse — God will deal with it as one deals with a perjurer.

verses.text_hebrew: חִצֵּ֣י גִבּ֣וֹר שְׁנוּנִ֑ים עִ֝֗ם גַּחֲלֵ֥י רְתָמִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Sharp arrows of the mighty, with coals of juniper.
verses.rendering:
Sharp arrows of a warrior,
with burning coals of broom wood.
verses.expanded_rendering: Chittzei gibbor shenunim ('sharp arrows of a mighty man') — the punishment for the deceitful tongue will be like warrior's arrows, precise and penetrating. The parallel image is gachalei retamim ('coals of broom wood'). The rotem ('broom plant') was known for producing exceptionally hot, long-lasting coals — they burn hotter and longer than other wood. The combination of arrows and coals creates a double image: the arrows pierce, and the coals burn. The deceitful tongue will receive a punishment that matches its own nature — just as lying words pierce and burn their victims, so God's judgment will pierce and burn the liar.
translator_notes: The retamim ('broom wood, broom plant') is identified with Retama raetam, a desert shrub whose roots produce charcoal of exceptional heat and duration. Bedouin traditions confirm that broom-wood coals retain heat far longer than other fuels. The imagery suggests a slow, thorough burning — not a flash fire but sustained heat.

verses.text_hebrew: אוֹיָה־לִ֭י כִּי־גַ֣רְתִּי מֶ֑שֶׁךְ שָׁ֝כַ֗נְתִּי עִֽם־אָהֳלֵ֥י קֵדָֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: Woe is me, that I sojourn in Mesech, that I dwell in the tents of Kedar!
verses.rendering:
What misery for me,
that I live as a foreigner in Meshech,
that I dwell among the tents of Kedar!
verses.expanded_rendering: Oyah li ki garti Meshekh shakhanti im oholei Qedar ('woe to me that I sojourn in Meshech, that I dwell with the tents of Kedar') — the psalmist names two locations at the extremes of the known world. Meshech was in far northern Anatolia (associated with the peoples of Genesis 10:2 and the hostile Gog of Ezekiel 38:2-3). Kedar was an Arabian tribal group in the southern and eastern deserts (Genesis 25:13, Isaiah 21:16). To be in both places at once is geographically impossible — the names function as metaphors for maximum distance from Jerusalem, from the Temple, from peace. The psalmist is saying: I am as far from home as it is possible to be.
translator_notes: The verb garti ('I sojourned') means to live as a resident alien, without permanent rights or status. The verb shakhanti ('I dwelt') is the same root as the Shekhinah (divine presence) and the mishkan (tabernacle) — but here the psalmist dwells not in God's presence but among those hostile to God's people. The contrast is painful.

key_terms.semantic_range: a nomadic Arabian tribe descended from Ishmael; symbolically represents distant, hostile territory
key_terms.note: Kedar was known for its skilled archers and warriors. Their black tents (Song of Solomon 1:5) became proverbial. As a metaphor in this psalm, Kedar represents life among people who are alien to the covenant community.

verses.text_hebrew: רַ֭בַּת שָֽׁכְנָה־לָּ֣הּ נַפְשִׁ֑י עִ֝֗ם שׂוֹנֵ֥א שָׁלֽוֹם׃
verses.text_kjv: My soul hath long dwelt with him that hateth peace.
verses.rendering:
Too long my soul has lived
among those who hate peace.
translator_notes: Rabbat shakhenah lah nafshi im soneh shalom ('too long has my soul dwelt with those who hate peace') — the word rabbat ('much, too much, too long') expresses exhaustion. The psalmist has endured this exile beyond what is bearable. His neighbors are described as soneh shalom ('those who hate peace') — peace itself is what they oppose. The word shalom ('peace, wholeness, well-being') is the opposite of everything the psalmist's environment represents.

verses.text_kjv: I am for peace: but when I speak, they are for war.
verses.rendering:
I am for peace,
but when I speak, they choose war.
translator_notes: The stark brevity of the final line is powerful: ani shalom ('I — peace') versus hemmah lammilchamah ('they — for war'). The contrast is absolute. The psalmist does not elaborate or explain — the two realities are placed side by side and left to speak for themselves. The lack of resolution is deliberate: the Songs of Ascents will provide the resolution as the pilgrim moves progressively closer to Jerusalem across Psalms 121-134.

key_terms.semantic_range: peace, wholeness, well-being, completeness, harmony, prosperity
key_terms.note: shalom is far more than the absence of conflict. It describes a state of total well-being — material, relational, spiritual, and communal. The psalmist's identification with shalom (ani shalom, 'I am peace') makes him a living contradiction to his environment, which is defined by milchamah ('war').
